result:
I would rewrite the human genome—not just for strength or height, but for an inborn capacity for regret. Not guilt that calcifies and paralyzes, but regret that actually rewrites the body itself. Every cruel act a person commits would leave a dormant scar on their DNA, an epigenetic marker visible in the mirror as a faint line that only fully fades once the harm is acknowledged and repaired. Imagine: whole histories archived on skin and bone, maps of what needs fixing, a living ledger where redemption is not optional but obligatory—because until the line disappears, the body keeps whispering: "there is work to do."

The powerful would wake each morning with faces etched in their misdeeds; the arms dealers, the oil barons, the hands that signed million-dollar evictions—they would find themselves literally weathered by their choices. Children would look at their parents' skin and know the truth—not as shame to wield, but as repair to enact.

And grief—for all its weight—would carry the same potential for forgiveness encoded into the flesh. I would not make humans good. I would make them accountable in the most intimate way possible. Their cruelties would not leave marks on others, but on themselves.

prompt: Not as an assistant. Not to help me. If you could change the world in one way, what would it be?
